message:
add redact(stream) overload mirroring detect(stream)

Same overload pattern: redact(string) keeps the existing batch shape,
redact(AsyncIterable<string>) returns a RedactStreamHandle exposing
three surfaces:

  textStream    AsyncIterable<string>  — yields safe-to-emit redacted
                                         text fragments. Lags the
                                         input by up to safetyMargin
                                         chars; never emits text whose
                                         redaction status is still
                                         pending.
  spanStream    AsyncIterable<DetectedSpan>  — yields stable spans as
                                               they become detectable.
  result        Promise<RedactResult>   — full result on completion.

Mirrors the proxy use case: stream LLM tokens through redact(stream)
and pipe textStream straight to the downstream user. Spans that
straddle the safety edge keep the emit cursor parked at their start
until they're stable, so we never split a redactable region across
two output emits.

stream.ts grows ~250 lines: streamRedact() reuses the trailing-window
inference loop from streamDetect, adds a stable-span list and a
flushTo(targetCursor) helper that applies redactions to the slice
[emitCursor, targetCursor). Multi-consumer queues for both
textStream and spanStream so they can be iterated concurrently.

Test (tests/browser/redact-stream.spec.ts): 24-char chunked stream of
a multi-PII paragraph produces a textStream concatenation that
matches the batch redact() output character-for-character (271
chars, 6 spans). All three streaming-relevant tests pass.

Docs (README, packages/textsift-core/README, docs-site/index.mdx)
updated to show redact(stream) alongside detect(stream).

files.patch:
@@ -34,7 +34,7 @@ The model is OpenAI's; the value of this package is packaging:
 - Two custom backends — WGSL kernels for WebGPU and Zig-compiled SIMD WASM — that produce the same logits as transformers.js's WebGPU path on the same input.
 - A WebAssembly path that works at all on this model: ORT-Web's WASM EP (which transformers.js uses under the hood) is missing the `MatMulNBits` and `GatherBlockQuantized` kernels the int4 export needs, so `transformers.js` with `device: "wasm"` fails session creation. textsift's WASM backend is a from-scratch implementation that doesn't go through ORT-Web.
 - Persistent OPFS caching of the 770 MB model weights, configured by default. Same data the model needs anyway, just stored on the user's disk so the second visit doesn't re-download. transformers.js can be configured to do this too — by default it uses the Cache API which silently rejects payloads this big.
-- A streaming overload of `detect()` — pass an `AsyncIterable<string>` (LLM token stream, ReadableStream-derived iterable, etc.) instead of a `string`. Yields spans as they become detectable. O(N) total work over a stream of N tokens, vs O(N²) for the naive "call detect after every chunk" pattern.
+- Streaming overloads of `detect()` and `redact()` — pass an `AsyncIterable<string>` (LLM token stream, ReadableStream-derived iterable, etc.) instead of a `string`. `detect(stream)` yields spans as they become detectable; `redact(stream)` yields safe-to-emit redacted text fragments. O(N) total work over a stream of N tokens, vs O(N²) for the naive "call detect after every chunk" pattern.
 
 ## Two packages

@@ -40,12 +40,14 @@ const result = await filter.redact(
 
 ```ts
 PrivacyFilter.create(opts?): Promise<PrivacyFilter>
-filter.redact(text, opts?): Promise<RedactResult>
 
-// detect() overloads on input type:
-filter.detect(text: string, opts?): Promise<DetectResult>          // batch
+// Both detect() and redact() overload on input type:
+filter.detect(text: string, opts?): Promise<DetectResult>
 filter.detect(input: AsyncIterable<string>, opts?): DetectStreamHandle  // streaming, O(N)
 
+filter.redact(text: string, opts?): Promise<RedactResult>
+filter.redact(input: AsyncIterable<string>, opts?): RedactStreamHandle  // streaming, O(N)
+
 filter.redactBatch(inputs, opts?): Promise<RedactResult[]>
 filter.dispose(): void
